rationale: This reaction is a classic textbook example of the Ritter reaction, demonstrating the formation of a stable tertiary carbocation from tert-butanol under strong acid conditions (sulfuric acid), followed by nucleophilic attack by acetonitrile. The resulting nitrilium ion intermediate is then hydrolyzed to form the final product, N-tert-butylacetamide. All reactants and products are simple, acyclic, and contain minimal functional groups, clearly illustrating the core transformation of an alcohol and a nitrile into an N-substituted amide.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses simple, acyclic aliphatic substrates (tert-butanol and acetonitrile) to avoid overlap with other named reactions. It avoids arenes (Friedel-Crafts, etc.), complex ring systems, or other reactive functional groups that could trigger unrelated reaction family classifications. The core transformation is clearly the C-N bond formation between a carbocation source and a nitrile, which is the defining feature of the Ritter reaction.
